name: leek pepper lentil skillet
instructions:
Sauté leeks, garlic, and thyme in olive oil until tender — but not fully sauteed the way you would for a soffrito. The leeks should soften and pick up flavor, not collapse.

Add raw lentils to the pan and cook long enough to drive off the funky lentil-water character.

Stir in fresh cucumbers OR bell peppers of any color (orange recommended). If using bell peppers, add a tiny bit of dijon mustard. If using cucumbers, no dijon — let the cucumber stay clean.

Season with salt and black pepper. Don't add other herbs. No cream. No vinegar.
notes:
A clean mediterranean lentil dish that needs neither stock nor tomatoes — but it isn't a stew. The skillet format keeps it dry; the leek + thyme combination carries the flavor without any of the usual lentil-stew supports.

FRESH VEGETABLE FORK: cucumbers OR bell peppers, not both. The two pull the dish in different directions:
- CUCUMBERS: lean and clean. The cucumber stays crisp and adds a cool fresh contrast to the warm lentils. No dijon needed; cucumber carries its own freshness.
- BELL PEPPERS (orange recommended): sweeter and more substantial. With bell peppers, a tiny bit of dijon mustard adds the bright sharpness that the bell pepper alone doesn't quite deliver. Without the dijon, the bell-pepper version reads a little flat.

LEEK + THYME: this dish is one of the best uses of the leek-thyme combination outside of soup contexts. Along with mexican cooking, leek-and-thyme mediterranean skillets are where thyme really earns its keep.

NO STOCK, NO TOMATOES, NO OTHER HERBS, NO CREAM: deliberate. Stock pushes toward a chicken-and-vegetable stew; tomatoes push italian; other herbs muddy the leek-thyme axis; cream pushes french. Holding all of these out is what keeps the dish in its own distinct mediterranean territory.

RED WINE VINEGAR: traditionally used in mediterranean lentil salads like this one — but I don't particularly love it here. The dish is built around the leek-thyme axis and the freshness of the cucumber or bell pepper, and vinegar competes with both rather than supporting them. Keeping it as a secondary acknowledgment that it's the canonical move, even if it's not my preference.
